sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
A physical chemistry lab studies cryopreservation solutions for cells. Solution A contains 0.20 m glucose. Solution B contains 0.20 m NaCl and is assumed to dissociate ideally. Solution C contains 0.20 m CaCl₂ and is also treated ideally for the initial calculation. Freezing-point depression is modeled as ΔT_f = iK_fm, where i is the van't Hoff factor.

Solution | Solute | Molality | Ideal i
A | glucose | 0.20 m | 1
B | NaCl | 0.20 m | 2
C | CaCl₂ | 0.20 m | 3

The investigators then cool a vial of pure water and a vial containing Solution B, recording the internal temperature over time (Figure 1). Cells placed in hypertonic solution shrink before freezing because water exits osmotically.
questions.stem: Which statement correctly identifies the samples in Figure 1 and explains their behavior?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sample A is Solution B, because its flat plateau indicates a higher heat capacity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sample A is pure water, because its flat plateau indicates that the phase change occurs at a constant temperature.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sample B is pure water, because its sloping plateau indicates the gradual release of latent heat.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sample B is Solution B, because its freezing point increases as the solvent freezes out.

explanations.A: A flat freezing plateau is characteristic of a pure substance undergoing a phase change at a constant temperature, not a solution with higher heat capacity. Solution B contains dissolved NaCl and should freeze over a range as the remaining liquid becomes more concentrated.
explanations.B: Correct. Sample A shows a relatively flat plateau near 0°C, identifying it as pure water. During the liquid-to-solid phase change, heat removal goes into forming the solid phase rather than lowering temperature, so the temperature remains nearly constant until freezing is complete.
explanations.C: The sloping region is not the pattern expected for pure water. It is more consistent with a solution: as pure solvent freezes out, the remaining liquid becomes more concentrated, so its freezing point continues to decrease during the freezing process.
explanations.D: Sample B is the solution, but the explanation gives the direction wrong. As solvent freezes out, the remaining solution becomes more concentrated, so its freezing point is depressed further rather than increased. That changing freezing point produces the sloped plateau.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Cooling curves for two aqueous samples. Sample A: 0.20 m glucose. Sample B: 0.20 m NaCl (complete dissociation assumed). Dotted line marks 0 °C.

questions.stem: Why do hypertonic cryoprotectant solutions cause cells to shrink before freezing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Water moves out of cells toward the higher extracellular solute concentration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Solute-free water actively pumps itself into cells.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypertonic solutions eliminate the plasma membrane.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Osmosis stops whenever temperature decreases.

explanations.A: Correct. A hypertonic extracellular solution has lower water potential than the cytosol, so water leaves cells osmotically. Cell volume falls before freezing. This is the same osmotic principle tested in physiology, applied to a physical chemistry context.
explanations.B: Water movement is not described as active pumping by water itself. Osmosis is passive movement driven by chemical potential gradients across a semipermeable membrane.
explanations.C: The solution changes water movement across the membrane; it does not eliminate the membrane. If the membrane were destroyed, controlled osmotic shrinkage would not be the right description.
explanations.D: Cooling can slow molecular motion, but osmosis does not instantly stop merely because temperature decreases. The passage describes shrinkage before freezing, so water movement is still occurring.

questions.stem: What does the flat plateau during pure water freezing represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sample has reached absolute zero.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No heat is removed during the plateau.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The water is becoming a gas.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Temperature stays constant while heat removal drives the liquid-to-solid phase change.

explanations.A: Absolute zero is far below ordinary freezing and cannot be inferred from a plateau near water's freezing point. The plateau is a phase-change signature, not a temperature minimum.
explanations.B: Heat must still be removed for freezing to proceed. The plateau means heat removal is not lowering temperature at that moment; it is driving the phase transition.
explanations.C: Freezing is liquid to solid, not liquid to gas. A gas-forming transition would be vaporization or sublimation depending on starting phase.
explanations.D: Correct. During a phase change, energy removal goes into changing intermolecular organization rather than lowering temperature. The plateau reflects latent heat of fusion being removed as liquid water becomes ice.

questions.stem: If Solution B behaves nonideally with an effective i less than 2, what is the most likely explanation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: NaCl becomes a covalent gas immediately.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ion pairing reduces the number of independently dissolved particles.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Molality becomes identical to temperature.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The freezing point no longer depends on particles.

explanations.A: NaCl does not become a covalent gas in aqueous cryopreservation solution. The deviation is about ionic interactions in solution, not a phase or bonding transformation into gas.
explanations.B: Correct. In real solutions, oppositely charged ions can interact or pair, lowering the effective number of independent solute particles. The measured colligative effect can therefore be smaller than the ideal van't Hoff prediction.
explanations.C: Molality and temperature are different quantities with different units. Nonideal behavior changes effective particle number; it does not redefine concentration as temperature.
explanations.D: Colligative properties still depend on particle number. Nonideality means the effective particle count differs from ideal dissociation, not that the particle concept disappears.

questions.stem: Which statement best explains why glucose can protect cells despite a smaller i than salts?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Glucose contributes osmotic particles without adding ionic strength that may disrupt cells.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only ionic solutes can affect freezing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glucose raises the freezing point by becoming ice.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glucose prevents all water molecules from moving.

explanations.A: Correct. Glucose supplies dissolved particles and can alter water chemical potential without producing multiple ions or high ionic strength. Cryoprotectant design often balances colligative benefit against cellular toxicity and osmotic stress.
explanations.B: Nonionic solutes also affect colligative properties because particle number matters, not charge alone. Glucose has i = 1 but still contributes osmotic and freezing-point effects.
explanations.C: Glucose does not protect by becoming ice or raising the freezing point. As a solute, it depresses freezing point, though less per mole than an ideally dissociating salt.
explanations.D: Water movement is reduced or redirected by osmotic gradients, not absolutely prevented. Cells can still gain or lose water depending on solution composition and membrane permeability.

questions.passage:
A second experiment compares vapor pressure and boiling point in volatile and nonvolatile solute solutions. A sealed chamber contains pure water in one compartment and an aqueous sucrose solution in another. At the same temperature, the sucrose solution has lower vapor pressure. When heated at 1 atm, the sucrose solution boils at a slightly higher temperature than pure water.

Sample | Solute type | Vapor pressure | Boiling point
Pure water | none | highest | 100.0°C
Sucrose solution | nonvolatile | lower | >100.0°C
Ethanol-water mixture | volatile solute | composition-dependent | variable

The instructor emphasizes that boiling occurs when vapor pressure equals external pressure. Students are asked to distinguish boiling-point elevation from heat capacity and from chemical reaction with solute.
questions.stem: Why does the sucrose solution have a higher boiling point than pure water?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sucrose eliminates atmospheric pressure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sucrose chemically converts water into ethanol.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Boiling occurs when vapor pressure is zero.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The nonvolatile solute lowers water's vapor pressure, so a higher temperature is needed to reach 1 atm.

explanations.A: Atmospheric pressure remains 1 atm in the experiment. The solute changes the solution's vapor pressure curve, not the external pressure applied to the liquid.
explanations.B: Sucrose does not convert water into ethanol under the described conditions. The boiling-point change is a physical colligative effect, not a chemical reaction.
explanations.C: Boiling occurs when vapor pressure equals external pressure, not when vapor pressure is zero. If vapor pressure were zero, no boiling would occur at ordinary pressures.
explanations.D: Correct. A nonvolatile solute lowers the mole fraction and escaping tendency of water, reducing vapor pressure at a given temperature. The solution must be heated more before its vapor pressure equals external pressure, producing boiling-point elevation.

questions.stem: In the sealed chamber, which way will net water transfer tend to occur initially?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No transfer because vapor pressure differs

choices.label: B
choices.text: From sucrose solution to pure water only

choices.label: C
choices.text: From pure water vapor toward the sucrose solution

choices.label: D
choices.text: From neither compartment because sucrose is volatile

explanations.A: A vapor pressure difference is exactly what drives net transfer in a sealed system. Equilibrium would require equal water chemical potential, not ignoring the difference.
explanations.B: This reverses the vapor-pressure gradient. The sucrose solution has lower vapor pressure, so it contributes less water vapor at the same temperature.
explanations.C: Correct. Pure water has higher vapor pressure, so more water molecules enter the gas phase from that side. The sucrose solution has lower water chemical potential, favoring condensation there. Net transfer tends to dilute the sucrose solution.
explanations.D: Sucrose is described as nonvolatile. It remains in solution and lowers water vapor pressure; it does not evaporate substantially to equalize compartments.

questions.stem: What would happen to boiling point if external pressure were lowered below 1 atm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Boiling point would decrease.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Boiling point would increase without limit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Boiling would require vapor pressure above 2 atm.

choices.label: D
choices.text: External pressure has no relation to boiling.

explanations.A: Correct. Boiling occurs when vapor pressure equals external pressure. If external pressure is lower, the liquid reaches that pressure at a lower temperature. This is why water boils below 100°C at high altitude.
explanations.B: Lower pressure makes boiling easier, not harder. An increased boiling point would be expected with higher external pressure, such as in a pressure cooker.
explanations.C: There is no requirement to exceed 2 atm when external pressure is below 1 atm. The equality condition uses the actual external pressure.
explanations.D: External pressure is central to boiling. The passage explicitly defines boiling by equality between vapor pressure and external pressure.

questions.stem: Why is the ethanol-water mixture more complicated than sucrose solution?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ethanol is nonvolatile and never evaporates.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ethanol is volatile and contributes to the vapor phase.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ethanol removes all hydrogen bonding from water.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ethanol makes vapor pressure undefined.

explanations.A: Ethanol is volatile; that is why ethanol-water mixtures require more careful interpretation. Treating it like sucrose would ignore ethanol's vapor contribution.
explanations.B: Correct. A volatile solute can evaporate and contribute its own partial pressure, so total vapor pressure depends on both composition and intermolecular interactions. That is different from a nonvolatile solute, which mainly lowers the solvent's vapor pressure.
explanations.C: Ethanol changes hydrogen-bonding patterns but does not remove all hydrogen bonding from water. The key distinction in the table is volatility, not total loss of intermolecular forces.
explanations.D: Vapor pressure remains definable. It is simply composition-dependent and may deviate from ideality. Complexity is not the same as absence of a measurable pressure.

questions.stem: Which property is colligative for a dilute nonvolatile solute?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nuclear spin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Molecular color

choices.label: C
choices.text: Freezing-point depression

choices.label: D
choices.text: Identity of covalent bonds in water

explanations.A: Nuclear spin is an intrinsic nuclear property and is not a colligative property. It is relevant to spectroscopy such as NMR, not freezing-point changes.
explanations.B: Color depends on electronic structure, not simply the number of particles. Two solutions with the same particle concentration can have very different colors.
explanations.C: Correct. Freezing-point depression depends primarily on the number of dissolved particles in dilute solution, not their detailed chemical identity. Boiling-point elevation, vapor-pressure lowering, and osmotic pressure are also classic colligative properties.
explanations.D: Colligative properties do not require changing water's covalent bonds. They arise from solution thermodynamics and particle number.
